food_subgroup: Matcha-based beverages with fruit and botanical additions

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for a composite grape-aloe-matcha beverage. FOODON_03301099 (matcha) is used as the closest single-ingredient anchor. The composite beverage nature is documented in classification.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Green tea) is the closest defensible category for a matcha-based beverage. Matcha is powdered green tea; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for flavoured matcha drinks with fruit and aloe.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite flavoured matcha beverage. SNOMED CT coverage for specialty beverage preparations is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g (one cup), typical for a flavoured matcha beverage served at a Japanese casual dining or café outlet in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Matcha (green tea powder) beverage, prepared with milk and sugar — estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central entries: Matcha green tea powder (FDC ID 2003571), Aloe vera juice (FDC ID 2261688), Grape juice (FDC ID 168155), and whole milk (FDC ID 746782)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry exists in any approved primary source for a commercial grape-aloe-matcha beverage. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composition: approximately 60% milk base, 15% grape juice component, 10% aloe vera bits, 10% matcha preparation, 5% added sugar. Values are derived from USDA FoodData Central approved entries for each component. This is a medium-confidence estimate. Sodium reflects typical commercial preparation. Values are expressed per 100 g of the final beverage.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021. Analogous estimate based on: sweetened milk-based matcha beverage (GI ~50–55, analogous to sweetened dairy-based tea drinks) and grape juice component (GI ~53, University of Sydney GI Database).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a commercial grape-aloe-matcha beverage. GI of 53 is estimated as a weighted average of the primary glycemic components: grape juice (GI 53, University of Sydney GI Database) and sweetened milk-based matcha (GI ~50, analogous to sweetened dairy tea, per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). Aloe vera bits contribute negligible glycemic load. The composite GI of 53 is classified as low (GI ≤55). Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (53 × 10.2 × 3.5) / 100 = 18.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~28 mg per 100 g; ~98 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 98 mg sodium per serving, this beverage is low in sodium and is generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension, though added sugar content should be monitored as excess sugar intake can indirectly elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (~9.5 g sugar per 100 g; ~33 g per 350 g serving) and estimated GI of 53
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 53 and approximately 33 g of sugar per serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this beverage in moderation, monitor portion size carefully, and account for the glycemic load of approximately 18.9 g per serving within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.9 g per 100 g) and low sodium; matcha contains catechins with potential cardioprotective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This beverage is low in saturated fat and sodium; matcha (Camellia sinensis) contains EGCG catechins associated with modest cardioprotective effects in peer-reviewed literature, though the clinical significance in a diluted commercial preparation is uncertain and added sugar should be limited in cardiovascular disease management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grape with Aloe Matcha is a Japanese-inspired flavoured matcha beverage served at casual dining outlets in Singapore, including Sukiya. It combines the earthy, umami-rich flavour of Shizuoka matcha powder — sourced from Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan's premier tea-growing region — with the sweet, fruity notes of grape and the mild, slightly gelatinous texture of aloe vera bits. The drink is typically prepared as a chilled or iced beverage with a milk or milk-alternative base, grape flavouring or juice, aloe vera gel pieces, matcha powder, and added sugar or syrup. The result is a layered sensory experience: the bitterness of high-quality matcha is balanced by the sweetness of grape and the refreshing, chewy texture of aloe bits. Nutritionally, per 350 g serving the beverage provides approximately 217 kcal, 6.3 g protein, 5.3 g fat, and 35.7 g carbohydrate, with an estimated glycemic index of 53 (low category) and a glycemic load of approximately 18.9 g. The matcha component contributes L-theanine and EGCG catechins, which have been associated with cognitive and cardiovascular benefits in peer-reviewed research. Clinically, the beverage is low in sodium and saturated fat, making it broadly suitable for most adults, but the sugar content warrants moderation for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. It is classified as a processed beverage and is vegetarian and gluten-free, though it likely contains dairy.

Is grape aloe matcha good for diabetes?
Grape matcha drink GI glycemic index
Japanese matcha drink with aloe bits

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA) for a commercial grape-aloe-matcha beverage. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component composition using USDA FoodData Central approved entries. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for grape juice and sweetened dairy-based tea beverages. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries (matcha powder FDC 2003571, aloe vera juice FDC 2261688, grape juice FDC 168155, whole milk FDC 746782) using an approximate weighted composition. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact commercial product. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero by formulation; dairy-based beverages at this scale contain negligible industrial trans fats and USDA data for the component ingredients reports 0.0 g trans fat. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for the component ingredients at the level of precision required for this composite estimate. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient precision across the component USDA FoodData Central entries used for this estimate. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for the primary components of this beverage. 6. glycemic_index of 53 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this commercial product was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 7. Allergen status (milk) is flagged as likely based on typical commercial matcha beverage preparation; exact formulation from Sukiya or Funan Mix & Match was not available from an approved source. 8. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese casual dining beverage portion; exact serving size was not confirmed from an approved nutritional source. 9. The food_name has been normalised from '727. Grape with Aloe Matcha' (menu-style with numbering) to 'Grape with Aloe Matcha' as the canonical English name per Veritas naming rules.
